# Numbers Chapter 13 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 162–164; Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 121–122 (LXX) | **Source Images:** `NUM_013_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_013_page_002.jpg`, `NUM_013_page_003.jpg`; `NUM_013_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_013_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

> **MANUSCRIPT OBSERVATION — THE TWELVE SPIES AND THE CRISIS OF FAITH:** Numbers 13 is the first half of the great reconnaissance narrative (chapters 13–14) that becomes the turning point of the entire wilderness generation. The chapter divides into four movements: YHWH's command to send men to spy out Canaan (vv. 1–3), the roster of the twelve tribal leaders chosen as spies (vv. 4–16), Moses' charge to the spies (vv. 17–20), the journey through the land (vv. 21–24), and the return and conflicting reports (vv. 25–33). On the Leningrad Codex, the chapter begins on page 162 (folio 84 recto), continuing from the itinerary notice of 12:16, and extends across pages 163 and 164. The setumah (ס) marker following 12:16 provides the break before the new parashah shelakh-lekha (שְׁלַח־לְךָ, "send for yourself"), which begins at 13:1. The Vaticanus preserves this chapter across pages 121–122. A significant structural difference exists between the MT and LXX versifications: the LXX numbers the spy roster beginning at 13:4 (as in the MT) but inserts the reconnaissance of the wilderness of Zin and Rehob before the tribal list, resulting in a different verse numbering in the middle portion. The translation below follows the MT versification of the Leningrad Codex throughout. The chapter's central tension is between what the spies see — a land genuinely flowing with milk and honey — and how they interpret what they see. The land is good; the question is whether Israel trusts the God who promised it.

---

## YHWH's Command and the Sending of the Spies (vv. 1–3)

**1** And-spoke [va-yedabber] YHWH unto [el] Moses [Mosheh], saying [le'mor]:

> **Key terms:** וַיְדַבֵּר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה לֵּאמֹר (va-yedabber YHWH el Mosheh le'mor) = "and YHWH spoke to Moses, saying" — the standard divine communication formula. This is the piel wayyiqtol of דבר (davar, "to speak"), the formal verb for divine instruction (as distinct from אמר, "to say," which introduces direct speech). The chapter opens with YHWH initiating the spy mission — a critical detail, since in the Deuteronomy 1:22–23 retelling, Moses attributes the initiative to the people. The MT presents the mission as divinely commanded; Deuteronomy presents it as a popular request that Moses approved. These are not contradictory but complementary perspectives: the people asked, and YHWH authorized. **LXX:** καὶ ἐλάλησεν κύριος πρὸς Μωυσῆν λέγων ("and [the] Lord spoke to Moses, saying").

**2** "Send [shelakh] for-yourself [lekha] men [anashim], and-let-them-spy-out [ve-yaturu] the-land of [et-erets] Canaan [Kena'an], which [asher] I [ani] [am] giving [noten] to-the-Sons of [li-vnei] Israel [Yisra'el]; one [ekhad] man [ish], one [ekhad] man [ish] for-the-tribe of [le-matteh] his-fathers [avotav] you-shall-send [tishla'khu], every-one [kol] a-prince [nasi] among-them [bahem]."

> **Key terms:** שְׁלַח־לְךָ (shelakh lekha) = "send for yourself" — the imperative of שׁלח (shalakh, "to send") with the ethical dative לְךָ (lekha, "for yourself"), which gives the command a nuanced force: "send on your own behalf / at your discretion." Rashi interprets: "according to your own judgment — I am not commanding you; if you wish, send." This reading reconciles the MT (YHWH commands) with Deuteronomy (the people request): YHWH grants permission rather than issuing an absolute order. וְיָתֻרוּ (ve-yaturu) = "and let them spy out / and let them explore" — from תור (tur, "to search, spy out, explore"), the characteristic verb of this chapter (appearing seven times: vv. 2, 16, 17, 21, 25, 32 [x2]). The verb implies not mere observation but evaluative reconnaissance — surveying with a purpose. אֶת־אֶרֶץ כְּנַעַן (et erets Kena'an) = "the land of Canaan" — the object marker אֶת plus the construct chain. כְּנַעַן (Kena'an) derives from the root כנע, possibly meaning "lowland" or connected to the term for merchants/traders (cf. Isa 23:8; Zech 14:21). אֲשֶׁר אֲנִי נֹתֵן (asher ani noten) = "which I am giving" — the participle נֹתֵן (noten, "giving") is in the present tense, indicating that the gift is ongoing and certain — not "which I will give" but "which I am [in the act of] giving." אִישׁ אֶחָד אִישׁ אֶחָד לְמַטֵּה אֲבֹתָיו (ish ekhad ish ekhad le-matteh avotav) = "one man, one man for the tribe of his fathers" — the distributive repetition "one man, one man" means "each one, one per [tribe]." נָשִׂיא (nasi) = "prince/chief/leader" — from נשׂא (nasa, "to lift up, carry"), one who is "lifted up" above others, a tribal leader. כֹּל נָשִׂיא בָהֶם (kol nasi bahem) = "every one a prince among them" — the spies are not scouts drawn from the ranks but tribal aristocrats, chieftains. **LXX:** ἀπόστειλον σεαυτῷ ἄνδρας, καὶ κατασκεψάσθωσαν τὴν γῆν τῶν Χαναναίων ("send for yourself men, and let them spy out the land of the Canaanites") — the LXX uses κατασκέπτομαι (kataskeptomai, "to spy out/reconnoiter") rather than a cognate of תור, shifting the nuance slightly toward military reconnaissance.

**3** And-sent [va-yishlakh] them [otam] Moses [Mosheh] from-the-wilderness of [mi-midbar] Paran [Pa'ran] by [al] the-mouth of [pi] YHWH; all of them [kullam] men [anashim], heads of [rashei] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el] they [hem].

> **Key terms:** עַל־פִּי יְהוָה (al pi YHWH) = "by the mouth of YHWH" — literally "upon the mouth of YHWH," the idiom for acting under divine authority or command (cf. Num 3:16, 39, 51; 4:37). Moses sends the spies with YHWH's explicit authorization. מִמִּדְבַּר פָּארָן (mi-midbar Pa'ran) = "from the wilderness of Paran" — the staging area established in 12:16 as the camp's location after leaving Khatserot. כֻּלָּם אֲנָשִׁים (kullam anashim) = "all of them men" — the plural אֲנָשִׁים (anashim) here carries the sense of "men of standing/importance," not merely "males" (cf. the distinction between אִישׁ [ish, "man of rank"] and אָדָם [adam, "human being"]). רָאשֵׁי בְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל הֵמָּה (rashei venei Yisra'el hemmah) = "heads of the Sons of Israel [were] they" — רֹאשׁ (rosh, "head") denotes a leading figure within the tribal hierarchy. The emphasis on the spies' status — they are princes, men of standing, heads — heightens the tragedy of their failure: it was not cowards or commoners who lost faith, but the nation's own leaders. **LXX:** καὶ ἐξαπέστειλεν αὐτοὺς Μωυσῆς ἐκ τῆς ἐρήμου Φαραν διὰ φωνῆς κυρίου· πάντες ἄνδρες ἀρχηγοὶ υἱῶν Ισραηλ οὗτοι ("and Moses sent them out from the wilderness of Pharan by the voice of [the] Lord; all men, leaders of [the] sons of Israel, [were] these") — the LXX renders רָאשֵׁי as ἀρχηγοί (archēgoi, "leaders/chieftains").

---

## The Roster of the Twelve Spies (vv. 4–16)

**4** And-these [ve-elleh] their-names [shemotam]: for-the-tribe of [le-matteh] Reuben [Re'uven], Shammua [Shammua] son of [ben] Zaccur [Zakkur].

> **Key terms:** וְאֵלֶּה שְׁמוֹתָם (ve-elleh shemotam) = "and these [were] their names" — the demonstrative pronoun אֵלֶּה (elleh, "these") plus the noun with possessive suffix שְׁמוֹתָם (shemotam, "their names"). This formula introduces a formal roster (cf. Num 1:5; 2 Sam 23:8). שַׁמּוּעַ בֶּן־זַכּוּר (Shammua ben Zakkur) — שַׁמּוּעַ (Shammua) = "heard/renowned," from שׁמע (shama, "to hear"); זַכּוּר (Zakkur) = "remembered," from זכר (zakhar, "to remember"). The tribe of Reuben is listed first as the firstborn tribe (cf. Num 1:5). **LXX:** τῆς φυλῆς Ρουβην Σαμουηλ υἱὸς Ζαχουρ ("of the tribe of Reuben, Samouel son of Zachour") — the LXX renders שַׁמּוּעַ as Σαμουηλ (Samouel), assimilating the name toward the more familiar "Samuel."

**5** For-the-tribe of [le-matteh] Simeon [Shim'on], Shaphat [Shafat] son of [ben] Hori [Khori].

> **Key terms:** שָׁפָט בֶּן־חוֹרִי (Shafat ben Khori) — שָׁפָט (Shafat) = "he judged," from שׁפט (shafat, "to judge"); חוֹרִי (Khori) = possibly "cave-dweller" or "noble/free-born" (cf. the Horites of Gen 36:20–30). **LXX:** τῆς φυλῆς Συμεων Σαφατ υἱὸς Σουρι ("of the tribe of Simeon, Saphat son of Souri").

**6** For-the-tribe of [le-matteh] Judah [Yehudah], Caleb [Kalev] son of [ben] Jephunneh [Yefunneh].

> **Key terms:** כָּלֵב בֶּן־יְפֻנֶּה (Kalev ben Yefunneh) — כָּלֵב (Kalev) is typically understood as meaning "dog" (from כֶּלֶב, kelev), though some scholars relate it to כָּל (kol, "all") + לֵב (lev, "heart"), yielding "wholehearted." Caleb will become, with Joshua, one of only two spies who give a faithful report. His tribe, Judah, is the royal tribe (cf. Gen 49:10). יְפֻנֶּה (Yefunneh) = possibly "he turned [to God]," from פנה (panah, "to turn"). **LXX:** τῆς φυλῆς Ιουδα Χαλεβ υἱὸς Ιεφοννη ("of the tribe of Judah, Chaleb son of Iephonne").

**7** For-the-tribe of [le-matteh] Issachar [Yissakhar], Igal [Yig'al] son of [ben] Joseph [Yosef].

> **Key terms:** יִגְאָל בֶּן־יוֹסֵף (Yig'al ben Yosef) — יִגְאָל (Yig'al) = "he redeems/will redeem," from גאל (ga'al, "to redeem"); יוֹסֵף (Yosef) = "he adds," from יסף (yasaf, "to add"). **LXX:** τῆς φυλῆς Ισσαχαρ Ιγαλ υἱὸς Ιωσηφ ("of the tribe of Issachar, Igal son of Ioseph").

**8** For-the-tribe of [le-matteh] Ephraim [Efrayim], Hoshea [Hoshe'a] son of [ben] Nun [Nun].

> **Key terms:** הוֹשֵׁעַ בֶּן־נוּן (Hoshe'a ben Nun) — הוֹשֵׁעַ (Hoshe'a) = "he saves/salvation," from ישׁע (yasha, "to save"). This is the original name of Joshua, before Moses renames him in v. 16. נוּן (Nun) = possibly "fish" or "posterity." Hoshea is the representative of the tribe of Ephraim, the leading tribe of the Joseph house. **LXX:** τῆς φυλῆς Εφραιμ Αυση υἱὸς Ναυη ("of the tribe of Ephraim, Ausē son of Nauē").

**9** For-the-tribe of [le-matteh] Benjamin [Binyamin], Palti [Palti] son of [ben] Raphu [Rafu].

> **Key terms:** פַּלְטִי בֶּן־רָפוּא (Palti ben Rafu) — פַּלְטִי (Palti) = "my deliverance," from פלט (palat, "to escape/deliver"); רָפוּא (Rafu) = "healed," from רפא (rafa, "to heal"). **LXX:** τῆς φυλῆς Βενιαμιν Φαλτι υἱὸς Ραφου ("of the tribe of Benjamin, Phalti son of Raphou").

**10** For-the-tribe of [le-matteh] Zebulun [Zevulun], Gaddiel [Gaddi'el] son of [ben] Sodi [Sodi].

> **Key terms:** גַּדִּיאֵל בֶּן־סוֹדִי (Gaddi'el ben Sodi) — גַּדִּיאֵל (Gaddi'el) = "God [is] my fortune" or "my fortune [is] God," from גַּד (gad, "fortune/luck") + אֵל (El, "God"); סוֹדִי (Sodi) = "my confidant" or "my council," from סוֹד (sod, "secret/counsel/council"). **LXX:** τῆς φυλῆς Ζαβουλων Γουδιηλ υἱὸς Σουδι ("of the tribe of Zaboulon, Goudiēl son of Soudi").

**11** For-the-tribe of [le-matteh] Joseph [Yosef], for-the-tribe of [le-matteh] Manasseh [Menasheh], Gaddi [Gaddi] son of [ben] Susi [Susi].

> **Key terms:** לְמַטֵּה יוֹסֵף לְמַטֵּה מְנַשֶּׁה (le-matteh Yosef le-matteh Menasheh) = "for the tribe of Joseph, for the tribe of Manasseh" — the double tribal designation clarifies that Manasseh represents the Joseph house alongside Ephraim (v. 8), since Joseph was divided into two tribes. גַּדִּי בֶּן־סוּסִי (Gaddi ben Susi) — גַּדִּי (Gaddi) = "my fortune," from גַּד (gad, "fortune"); סוּסִי (Susi) = "my horse" or "horseman," from סוּס (sus, "horse"). **LXX:** τῆς φυλῆς Ιωσηφ τῆς φυλῆς Μανασση Γαδδι υἱὸς Σουσι ("of the tribe of Joseph, of the tribe of Manasse, Gaddi son of Sousi").

**12** For-the-tribe of [le-matteh] Dan [Dan], Ammiel [Ammi'el] son of [ben] Gemalli [Gemalli].

> **Key terms:** עַמִּיאֵל בֶּן גְּמַלִּי (Ammi'el ben Gemalli) — עַמִּיאֵל (Ammi'el) = "my people [is/belongs to] God" or "God [is] my kinsman," from עַם (am, "people/kinsman") + אֵל (El, "God"); גְּמַלִּי (Gemalli) = "my recompense" or "camel-driver," from either גמל (gamal, "to ripen/wean/recompense") or גָּמָל (gamal, "camel"). **LXX:** τῆς φυλῆς Δαν Αμιηλ υἱὸς Γαμαλι ("of the tribe of Dan, Amiēl son of Gamali").

**13** For-the-tribe of [le-matteh] Asher [Asher], Sethur [Setur] son of [ben] Michael [Mikha'el].

> **Key terms:** סְתוּר בֶּן־מִיכָאֵל (Setur ben Mikha'el) — סְתוּר (Setur) = "hidden," from סתר (satar, "to hide/conceal"); מִיכָאֵל (Mikha'el) = "who [is] like God?", from מִי (mi, "who?") + כְּ (ke, "like") + אֵל (El, "God"). **LXX:** τῆς φυλῆς Ασηρ Σαθουρ υἱὸς Μιχαηλ ("of the tribe of Asēr, Sathour son of Michaēl").

**14** For-the-tribe of [le-matteh] Naphtali [Naftali], Nahbi [Nakhbi] son of [ben] Vophsi [Vofsi].

> **Key terms:** נַחְבִּי בֶּן־וָפְסִי (Nakhbi ben Vofsi) — נַחְבִּי (Nakhbi) = "hidden/timid," possibly from חבא (khava, "to hide [oneself]"); וָפְסִי (Vofsi) = obscure, possibly from a root meaning "to add" or "to vanish." **LXX:** τῆς φυλῆς Νεφθαλι Ναβι υἱὸς Ιαβι ("of the tribe of Nephtali, Nabi son of Iabi") — the LXX diverges considerably in the rendering of this father's name.

**15** For-the-tribe of [le-matteh] Gad [Gad], Geuel [Ge'u'el] son of [ben] Machi [Makhi].

> **Key terms:** גְּאוּאֵל בֶּן־מָכִי (Ge'u'el ben Makhi) — גְּאוּאֵל (Ge'u'el) = "majesty of God" or "God has lifted up," from גאה (ga'ah, "to rise, grow, be exalted") + אֵל (El, "God"); מָכִי (Makhi) = "my smitten [one]" or "my reduction," possibly from מכה (makhah, "to strike"). **LXX:** τῆς φυλῆς Γαδ Γουδιηλ υἱὸς Μακχι ("of the tribe of Gad, Goudiēl son of Makchi").

**16** These [elleh] [are] the-names of [shemot] the-men [ha-anashim] whom [asher] sent [shalakh] Moses [Mosheh] to-spy-out [la-tur] the-land [et-ha-arets]. And-called [va-yiqra] Moses [Mosheh] Hoshea [Hoshe'a] son of [ben] Nun [Nun] — Joshua [Yehoshu'a].

> **Key terms:** וַיִּקְרָא מֹשֶׁה לְהוֹשֵׁעַ בִּן־נוּן יְהוֹשֻׁעַ (va-yiqra Mosheh le-Hoshe'a bin Nun Yehoshu'a) = "and Moses called Hoshea son of Nun — Joshua" — this is the renaming verse. הוֹשֵׁעַ (Hoshe'a) = "he saves" becomes יְהוֹשֻׁעַ (Yehoshu'a) = "YHWH saves" by the prefixing of the theophoric element יְהוֹ (Yeho-), the abbreviated form of the divine name YHWH. The renaming adds the divine name to the root meaning: salvation is not a generic act but YHWH's specific act. Moses renames his apprentice on the eve of the mission, as if to arm him with a theological confession: "YHWH is the one who saves." The timing is deliberate — before Joshua enters the land of promise, he receives a name that embeds Israel's theology of deliverance. **LXX:** καὶ ἐπωνόμασεν Μωυσῆς τὸν Αυση υἱὸν Ναυη Ἰησοῦν ("and Moses named Ausē son of Nauē — Iēsoun/Jesus") — the LXX renders יְהוֹשֻׁעַ as Ἰησοῦς (Iēsous), the same name later borne by Jesus of Nazareth in the New Testament. The name continuity is not accidental: the LXX translators used the same Greek form for both figures, and the author of Hebrews exploits this (Heb 4:8).

---

## Moses' Charge to the Spies (vv. 17–20)

**17** And-sent [va-yishlakh] them [otam] Moses [Mosheh] to-spy-out [la-tur] the-land of [et-erets] Canaan [Kena'an]; and-said [va-yomer] unto-them [alehem]: "Go-up [alu] this [zeh] [way] by-the-Negev [ba-Negev], and-go-up [va-alitem] the-mountain [et-ha-har].

> **Key terms:** עֲלוּ זֶה בַּנֶּגֶב (alu zeh ba-Negev) = "go up this [way] by the Negev" — the imperative עֲלוּ (alu, "go up!") with the demonstrative זֶה (zeh, "this"), functioning as a directional adverb ("this way / in this direction"). The נֶּגֶב (Negev) = "south / the dry land" — from a root meaning "to be dry," referring to the arid southern region of Canaan. The spies are to enter from the south — ascending from the wilderness of Paran through the Negev and up into the hill country. וַעֲלִיתֶם אֶת־הָהָר (va-alitem et-ha-har) = "and go up the mountain / the hill country" — הָהָר (ha-har) = "the mountain" or collectively "the hill country," referring to the central ridge of Canaan (the later territory of Judah and Ephraim). **LXX:** ἀνάβητε τοῦτο τῇ ἐρήμῳ καὶ ἀναβήσεσθε εἰς τὸ ὄρος ("go up this [way] through the wilderness and you shall go up into the mountain").

**18** And-see [u-re'item] the-land [et-ha-arets] — what [mah] it [hi] [is]; and-the-people [ve-et-ha-am] the-dwelling [ha-yoshev] upon-it [aleha] — [are they] strong [he-khazaq] or [hu] weak [ha-rafeh], [are they] few [ha-me'at] or [hu] many [ha-rav]?

> **Key terms:** וּרְאִיתֶם אֶת־הָאָרֶץ מַה־הִוא (u-re'item et-ha-arets mah hi) = "and you shall see the land — what it [is]" — the verb ראה (ra'ah, "to see") here means "to observe, to assess." Moses issues a series of paired interrogatives, structuring the reconnaissance mission around binary evaluations. הֶחָזָק הוּא הֲרָפֶה (he-khazaq hu ha-rafeh) = "is it [the people] strong or weak?" — the adjectives חָזָק (khazaq, "strong") and רָפֶה (rafeh, "weak/feeble") form the first pair. הַמְעַט הוּא אִם־רָב (ha-me'at hu im rav) = "is it [the people] few or many?" — the second pair. The interrogative particle הֲ (ha-) introduces yes-or-no questions, and the disjunctive הוּא (hu) / אִם (im) means "or." **LXX:** καὶ ὄψεσθε τὴν γῆν τίς ἐστιν, καὶ τὸν λαὸν τὸν ἐγκαθήμενον ἐπ᾽ αὐτῆς, εἰ ἰσχυρότερός ἐστιν ἢ ἀσθενής, εἰ ὀλίγοι εἰσὶν ἢ πολλοί ("and you shall see the land, what it is, and the people dwelling upon it, whether it is stronger or weak, whether few or many").

**19** And-what [u-mah] [is] the-land [ha-arets] which [asher] he [hu] [is] dwelling [yoshev] in-it [bah] — [is it] good [ha-tovah] it [hi] or [im] bad [ra'ah]; and-what [u-mah] [are] the-cities [he-arim] which [asher] he [hu] [is] dwelling [yoshev] in-them [ba-hennah] — [are they] in-camps [ha-be-makhanim] or [im] in-fortresses [be-mivtsarim]?

> **Key terms:** הַטּוֹבָה הִוא אִם־רָעָה (ha-tovah hi im ra'ah) = "is it good or bad?" — the third binary pair, assessing the quality of the land for habitation. הַבְּמַחֲנִים אִם בְּמִבְצָרִים (ha-be-makhanim im be-mivtsarim) = "in camps or in fortresses?" — the fourth pair, assessing the military infrastructure. מַחֲנִים (makhanim) = "camps" — open, unfortified encampments, suggesting a vulnerable population. מִבְצָרִים (mivtsarim) = "fortresses/fortified cities" — from בצר (batsar, "to cut off, make inaccessible, fortify"), implying walled cities with defensive works. **LXX:** καὶ τίς ἡ γῆ εἰς ἣν οὗτοι ἐγκάθηνται ἐπ᾽ αὐτῆς, εἰ καλή ἐστιν ἢ πονηρά, καὶ τίνες αἱ πόλεις εἰς ἃς οὗτοι κατοικοῦσιν ἐν αὐταῖς, εἰ ἐν τειχήρεσιν ἢ ἐν ἀτειχίστοις ("and what [is] the land in which these dwell upon it, whether good or evil, and what the cities in which these inhabit in them, whether in walled [cities] or in unwalled").

**20** And-what [u-mah] [is] the-land [ha-arets] — [is it] fat [ha-shemenah] it [hi] or [im] lean [razah]; is-there [ha-yesh] in-it [bah] a-tree [ets] or [im] not [ayin]? And-strengthen-yourselves [ve-hitkhazzaqtem] and-take [u-leqakhtem] from-the-fruit of [mi-peri] the-land [ha-arets]." And-the-days [ve-ha-yamim] [were the] days of [yemei] the-first-ripe [bikkurei] grapes [anavim].

> **Key terms:** הַשְּׁמֵנָה הִוא אִם־רָזָה (ha-shemenah hi im razah) = "is it fat or lean?" — the fifth binary pair. שְׁמֵנָה (shemenah, "fat/rich") from שׁמן (shaman, "to be fat"), describing fertile, productive soil; רָזָה (razah, "lean/thin"), describing poor, unproductive land. הֲיֵשׁ־בָּהּ עֵץ אִם־אַיִן (ha-yesh bah ets im ayin) = "is there a tree in it or not?" — the question about trees assesses both timber resources and agricultural viability; a well-treed land indicates rainfall, good soil, and established cultivation. וְהִתְחַזַּקְתֶּם (ve-hitkhazzaqtem) = "and strengthen yourselves / and be courageous" — the hitpael of חזק (khazaq, "to be strong"), an exhortation to boldness. The command to bring back fruit provides physical evidence of the land's fertility. וְהַיָּמִים יְמֵי בִּכּוּרֵי עֲנָבִים (ve-ha-yamim yemei bikkurei anavim) = "and the days were the days of the first-ripe grapes" — a temporal marker placing the mission in late summer (approximately July–August), when the first grapes ripen. בִּכּוּרִים (bikkurim) = "first fruits/first-ripe [produce]," from בכר (bakhar, "to be early/firstborn"). **LXX:** καὶ τίς ἡ γῆ εἰ πίων ἢ παρειμένη, εἰ ἔστιν ἐν αὐτῇ δένδρα ἢ οὔ· καὶ προσκαρτερήσαντες λήμψεσθε ἀπὸ τῶν καρπῶν τῆς γῆς. καὶ αἱ ἡμέραι ἡμέραι ἔαρος, πρόδρομοι σταφυλῆς ("and what the land, whether rich or slack, whether there are trees in it or not; and being bold you shall take from the fruits of the land. And the days [were] days of spring, forerunners of the grape") — the LXX renders בִּכּוּרֵי עֲנָבִים as πρόδρομοι σταφυλῆς ("forerunners of the grape"), a vivid equivalent.

---

## The Journey Through the Land (vv. 21–24)

**21** And-they-went-up [va-ya'alu] and-they-spied-out [va-yaturu] the-land [et-ha-arets] from-the-wilderness of [mi-midbar] Zin [Tsin] unto [ad] Rehob [Rekhov], at-the-entering of [li-vo] Hamath [Khamat].

> **Key terms:** מִמִּדְבַּר־צִן עַד־רְחֹב לְבֹא חֲמָת (mi-midbar Tsin ad Rekhov levo Khamat) = "from the wilderness of Zin unto Rehob, at the entering of Hamath" — this defines the full extent of the reconnaissance: from the extreme south (the wilderness of Zin, at the southern boundary of Canaan, near Kadesh) to the extreme north (Lebo-Hamath, the entrance to the Hamath valley in modern Lebanon/Syria). The spies traversed the entire land from south to north. רְחֹב (Rekhov) = "broad place/plaza," a town near the northern border; לְבֹא חֲמָת (levo Khamat) = "the entering/approach of Hamath," a standard designation for Canaan's northern boundary (cf. Num 34:8; Josh 13:5; Judg 3:3; 1 Kgs 8:65). **LXX:** καὶ ἀναβάντες κατεσκέψαντο τὴν γῆν ἀπὸ τῆς ἐρήμου Σιν ἕως Ρααβ εἰσπορευομένων Εφααθ ("and going up they spied out the land from the wilderness of Sin unto Raab at the entering of Ephaath").

**22** And-they-went-up [va-ya'alu] by-the-Negev [ba-Negev], and-he-came [va-yavo] unto [ad] Hebron [Khevron]; and-there [ve-sham] [were] Ahiman [Akhiman], Sheshai [Sheshai], and-Talmai [ve-Talmai], the-born-ones of [yelidei] the-Anak [ha-Anaq]. And-Hebron [ve-Khevron] [was] built [nivnetah] seven [sheva] years [shanim] before [li-fnei] Zoan [Tso'an] of-Egypt [Mitsrayim].

> **Key terms:** וַיָּבֹא (va-yavo) = "and he came" — the verb is third-person masculine singular, not plural (which would be וַיָּבֹאוּ, va-yavo'u). The singular may indicate that Caleb alone entered Hebron (cf. Josh 14:13–14; Judg 1:20, where Caleb is specifically associated with the conquest of Hebron), or it may be a collective singular referring to the group. The Talmud (Sotah 34b) interprets: "Caleb went alone to prostrate himself upon the graves of the patriarchs" at Machpelah. יְלִידֵי הָעֲנָק (yelidei ha-Anaq) = "the born-ones / descendants of the Anak" — the Anakim, a clan of exceptionally tall people, whose name derives from עֲנָק (anaq, "neck" or "necklace"), perhaps suggesting "long-necked ones" (i.e., tall people). אֲחִימַן שֵׁשַׁי וְתַלְמַי (Akhiman Sheshai ve-Talmai) — three named Anakite chieftains. אֲחִימַן (Akhiman) = "my brother is a gift" or "brother of Who?"; שֵׁשַׁי (Sheshai) = possibly from Egyptian origin; תַּלְמַי (Talmai) = "furrowed/abounding in furrows," from תֶּלֶם (telem, "furrow"). וְחֶבְרוֹן שֶׁבַע שָׁנִים נִבְנְתָה לִפְנֵי צֹעַן מִצְרָיִם (ve-Khevron sheva shanim nivnetah li-fnei Tso'an Mitsrayim) = "and Hebron was built seven years before Zoan of Egypt" — a parenthetical antiquarian note. Zoan (Tso'an) is the Hebrew name for Tanis (or possibly Avaris/Pi-Ramesses), an ancient Egyptian city in the eastern Delta. The note establishes Hebron's great antiquity — it is older than one of Egypt's premier cities. Hebron is the burial place of the patriarchs (Gen 23:19; 25:9; 49:30–31; 50:13) and later David's first capital (2 Sam 2:11). **LXX:** καὶ ἀνέβησαν κατὰ τὴν ἔρημον καὶ ἦλθον ἕως Χεβρων, καὶ ἐκεῖ Αχιμαν καὶ Σεσσι καὶ Θελαμιν γενεαὶ Εναχ· καὶ Χεβρων ἑπτὰ ἔτεσιν ᾠκοδομήθη πρὸ τοῦ Τάνιν Αἰγύπτου ("and they went up through the wilderness and came unto Hebron, and there [were] Achiman and Sessi and Thelamin, generations of Enach; and Hebron was built seven years before Tanis of Egypt") — the LXX renders צֹעַן as Τάνιν (Tanin/Tanis), providing the Egyptian equivalent.

**23** And-they-came [va-yavo'u] unto [ad] the-valley of [nakhal] Eshcol [Eshkol], and-they-cut [va-yikhretu] from-there [mi-sham] a-branch [zemorah] and-a-cluster of [ve-eshkol] grapes [anavim] one [ekhad]; and-they-carried [va-yissa'uhu] on-a-pole [ba-mot] between-two [bi-shnayim]. And-[also] from [u-min] the-pomegranates [ha-rimmonim] and-from [u-min] the-figs [ha-te'enim].

> **Key terms:** נַחַל אֶשְׁכֹּל (nakhal Eshkol) = "the valley/wadi of Eshcol" — נַחַל (nakhal) is a seasonal streambed or wadi; אֶשְׁכּוֹל (eshkol) = "cluster [of grapes]." The place will be named for the cluster (v. 24). זְמוֹרָה (zemorah) = "branch/vine-shoot" — a cutting from the grapevine, still bearing its fruit cluster. וַיִּשָּׂאֻהוּ בַמּוֹט בִּשְׁנָיִם (va-yissa'uhu va-mot bi-shnayim) = "and they carried it on a pole between two [men]" — the cluster was so large that it required a carrying-pole (מוֹט, mot) borne by two men. This vivid detail serves as physical proof of the land's extraordinary fertility — a single grape cluster too heavy for one man. The מוֹט (mot) is a wooden bar or pole, used for carrying heavy loads suspended between two porters (cf. the carrying of the ark, Num 4:10, 12). וּמִן־הָרִמֹּנִים וּמִן־הַתְּאֵנִים (u-min ha-rimmonim u-min ha-te'enim) = "and from the pomegranates and from the figs" — two additional fruits representing the agricultural richness of Canaan. These three fruits — grapes, pomegranates, and figs — appear together in the description of the land's bounty in Deuteronomy 8:8. **LXX:** καὶ ἤλθοσαν ἕως φάραγγος βότρυος, καὶ κατεσκέψαντο αὐτήν, καὶ ἔκοψαν ἐκεῖθεν κλῆμα καὶ βότρυν σταφυλῆς ἕνα ἐπ᾽ αὐτοῦ, καὶ ἦραν αὐτὸν ἐπ᾽ ἀναφορεῦσιν ἐν δυσίν, καὶ ἀπὸ τῶν ῥοῶν καὶ ἀπὸ τῶν συκῶν ("and they came to the ravine of the cluster, and spied it out, and cut from there a vine-branch and one cluster of grapes upon it, and carried it upon carrying-poles between two, and [took] from the pomegranates and from the figs").

**24** That-place [la-maqom] that [ha-hu] he-called [qara] the-valley of [nakhal] Eshcol [Eshkol], on-account of [al odot] the-cluster [ha-eshkol] which [asher] cut [kharetu] from-there [mi-sham] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el].

> **Key terms:** לַמָּקוֹם הַהוּא קָרָא נַחַל אֶשְׁכּוֹל (la-maqom ha-hu qara nakhal Eshkol) = "that place he called the Valley of Eshcol" — an etiological notice explaining the origin of the place name. The subject of קָרָא (qara, "he called") is impersonal ("one called" / "it was called"). The naming commemorates the extraordinary grape cluster. עַל אֹדוֹת הָאֶשְׁכּוֹל (al odot ha-eshkol) = "on account of the cluster" — the same prepositional phrase used in 12:1 (al odot, "on account of"). **LXX:** τὸν τόπον ἐκεῖνον ἐπωνόμασαν Φάραγξ βότρυος, διὰ τὸν βότρυν ὃν ἔκοψαν ἐκεῖθεν οἱ υἱοὶ Ισραηλ ("they named that place Ravine of the Cluster, because of the cluster which the sons of Israel cut from there").

## The Return and the Reports (vv. 25–33)

**25** And-they-returned [va-yashuvu] from-spying-out [mi-tur] the-land [ha-arets] at-the-end of [mi-qets] forty [arba'im] days [yom].

> **Key terms:** מִקֵּץ אַרְבָּעִים יוֹם (mi-qets arba'im yom) = "at the end of forty days" — the reconnaissance lasted forty days, a typologically significant number in the Hebrew Bible (cf. the flood — forty days, Gen 7:4; Moses on Sinai — forty days, Exod 24:18; Elijah's journey to Horeb — forty days, 1 Kgs 19:8). In 14:34, YHWH will decree one year of wilderness wandering for each day of the spying mission: forty days yields forty years. The number forty thus functions both literally (the duration of the journey) and proleptically (foreshadowing the punishment). **LXX:** καὶ ἐπέστρεψαν ἐκεῖθεν κατασκεψάμενοι τὴν γῆν μετὰ τεσσαράκοντα ἡμέρας ("and they returned from there, having spied out the land, after forty days").

**26** And-they-went [va-yelkhu] and-they-came [va-yavo'u] unto [el] Moses [Mosheh] and-unto [ve-el] Aaron [Aharon] and-unto [ve-el] all [kol] the-congregation of [adat] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el], unto [el] the-wilderness of [midbar] Paran [Pa'ran], unto [el] Kadesh [Qadesh]; and-they-brought-back [va-yashivu] them [otam] word [davar], and-[also] all [kol] the-congregation [ha-edah]; and-they-showed-them [va-yar'um] the-fruit of [et-peri] the-land [ha-arets].

> **Key terms:** וַיֵּלְכוּ וַיָּבֹאוּ (va-yelkhu va-yavo'u) = "and they went and they came" — the paired verbs express the completed journey of departure and return. The Midrash (Sotah 35a) reads the unusual pairing as a comparison: "just as their going was with evil intent, so their coming was with evil intent" — i.e., the ten spies had already determined to deliver a negative report before they left. קָדֵשׁ (Qadesh) = "holy/sacred place" — Kadesh (Kadesh-barnea), the great oasis in the northeastern Sinai/southern Negev, which serves as the base camp for the spying mission and the site of Israel's prolonged encampment during the wilderness years. מִדְבַּר פָּארָן (midbar Pa'ran) = "the wilderness of Paran" — the broad region encompassing Kadesh. וַיָּשִׁיבוּ אֹתָם דָּבָר (va-yashivu otam davar) = "and they brought back to them word/report" — the hiphil of שׁוב (shuv, "to return"), here meaning "to bring back [a report]." וְאֶת כָּל הָעֵדָה (ve-et kol ha-edah) = "and all the congregation" — the report is given publicly, to the entire assembly, not privately to Moses alone. This public setting amplifies the damage of the negative report: the entire community hears the fearful words. **LXX:** καὶ ἐπορεύθησαν καὶ ἤλθοσαν πρὸς Μωυσῆν καὶ Ααρων καὶ πρὸς πᾶσαν συναγωγὴν υἱῶν Ισραηλ εἰς τὴν ἔρημον Φαραν Καδης ("and they traveled and came to Moses and Aaron and to all the congregation of the sons of Israel, to the wilderness of Pharan, Kadesh").

**27** And-they-told [va-yesapperu] to-him [lo] and-said [va-yomeru]: "We-came [banu] unto [el] the-land [ha-arets] which [asher] you-sent-us [shelakhtanu]; and-also [ve-gam] flowing [zavat] [with] milk [khalav] and-honey [u-devash] it [hi]; and-this [ve-zeh] [is] its-fruit [piriyah].

> **Key terms:** וַיְסַפְּרוּ־לוֹ (va-yesapperu lo) = "and they recounted/told to him" — the piel of ספר (safar, "to count/recount/narrate"), indicating a detailed report. זָבַת חָלָב וּדְבָשׁ (zavat khalav u-devash) = "flowing [with] milk and honey" — the classic description of the promised land (cf. Exod 3:8, 17; 13:5; 33:3; Lev 20:24). The feminine participle זָבַת (zavat, "flowing") from זוב (zuv, "to flow/ooze") pictures the land as overflowing with pastoral and agricultural abundance. חָלָב (khalav) = "milk" — representing pastoral wealth (flocks and herds); דְּבָשׁ (devash) = "honey" — either bee-honey or the syrup of dates and other fruits, representing horticultural bounty. וְזֶה פִּרְיָהּ (ve-zeh piryah) = "and this [is] its fruit" — the spies present the physical evidence: the enormous grape cluster, the pomegranates, and the figs. The report begins positively — the land is indeed everything YHWH promised. **LXX:** καὶ διηγήσαντο αὐτῷ καὶ εἶπαν· ἤλθαμεν εἰς τὴν γῆν εἰς ἣν ἀπέστειλας ἡμᾶς, γῆν ῥέουσαν γάλα καὶ μέλι ("and they narrated to him and said: we came into the land to which you sent us — a land flowing [with] milk and honey").

**28** However [efes], strong [az] [is] the-people [ha-am] the-dwelling [ha-yoshev] in-the-land [ba-arets]; and-the-cities [ve-he-arim] [are] fortified [betsurot], very-great [gedolot me'od]; and-also [ve-gam] the-born-ones of [yelidei] the-Anak [ha-Anaq] we-saw [ra'inu] there [sham].

> **Key terms:** אֶפֶס (efes) = "however/but/only" — this is the pivotal word of the entire chapter. After affirming the land's goodness (v. 27), the spies introduce the devastating "but" that reverses the trajectory of their report. אֶפֶס literally means "end/extremity/nothing" (cf. Isa 45:22; 52:10); used as a conjunction, it introduces a limiting or adversative clause: "except that / only / however." The word marks the transition from faith-affirming observation to fear-driven interpretation. כִּי עַז הָעָם (ki az ha-am) = "for strong [is] the people" — עַז (az) = "strong/fierce/mighty," from עזז (azaz, "to be strong"). בְּצֻרוֹת גְּדֹלֹת מְאֹד (betsurot gedolot me'od) = "fortified, very great" — the cities are not merely walled but massively fortified. בְּצוּרָה (betsurah) = "fortified/inaccessible," from בצר (batsar, "to cut off/fortify"). וְגַם יְלִדֵי הָעֲנָק רָאִינוּ שָׁם (ve-gam yelidei ha-Anaq ra'inu sham) = "and also the descendants of the Anak we saw there" — the mention of the Anakim adds a note of terror. The Anakim, introduced in v. 22, were known as giants; their presence transforms the assessment from "difficult" to "impossible." **LXX:** ἀλλ᾽ ἢ ὅτι ἰσχυρὸν τὸ ἔθνος τὸ κατοικοῦν ἐπ᾽ αὐτῆς, καὶ αἱ πόλεις ὀχυραὶ τετειχισμέναι καὶ μεγάλαι σφόδρα, καὶ τὴν γενεὰν Εναχ ἑωράκαμεν ἐκεῖ ("but that strong [is] the nation dwelling upon it, and the cities [are] fortified, walled, and very great, and the race of Enach we saw there").

**29** Amalek [Amaleq] [is] dwelling [yoshev] in-the-land of [be-erets] the-Negev [ha-Negev]; and-the-Hittite [ve-ha-Khitti] and-the-Jebusite [ve-ha-Yevusi] and-the-Amorite [ve-ha-Emori] [are] dwelling [yoshev] in-the-mountain [ba-har]; and-the-Canaanite [ve-ha-Kena'ani] [is] dwelling [yoshev] by [al] the-sea [ha-yam] and-by [ve-al] the-bank of [yad] the-Jordan [ha-Yarden].

> **Key terms:** The verse maps the hostile population by geographical zone: עֲמָלֵק (Amaleq) controls the Negev (the southern desert approach — Israel's entry point); the Hittites (חִתִּי, Khitti), Jebusites (יְבוּסִי, Yevusi), and Amorites (אֱמֹרִי, Emori) hold the central hill country (הָהָר, ha-har); and the Canaanites (כְּנַעֲנִי, Kena'ani) control the Mediterranean coast and the Jordan Valley (עַל־הַיָּם וְעַל יַד הַיַּרְדֵּן, al ha-yam ve-al yad ha-Yarden). The effect is to present Canaan as encircled by enemies on every side: there is no unguarded approach, no gap in the defensive ring. Amalek's placement first is particularly threatening — they are Israel's hereditary enemy (Exod 17:8–16), blocking the southern entrance. **LXX:** καὶ Αμαληκ κατοικεῖ ἐν τῇ γῇ τῇ πρὸς νότον, καὶ ὁ Χετταῖος καὶ ὁ Ευαῖος καὶ ὁ Αμορραῖος καὶ ὁ Χαναναῖος κατοικεῖ ἐν τῇ ὀρεινῇ, καὶ ὁ Χαναναῖος κατοικεῖ παρὰ θάλασσαν καὶ παρὰ τὸν Ιορδάνην ποταμόν ("and Amalek dwells in the land toward the south, and the Hettite and the Euaite and the Amorite and the Canaanite dwell in the hill country, and the Canaanite dwells by the sea and by the river Jordan") — the LXX adds ὁ Ευαῖος (the Hivite) where the MT has the Jebusite, a variant in the traditional people-list.

**30** And-silenced [va-yahas] Caleb [Kalev] the-people [et-ha-am] unto [el] Moses [Mosheh], and-said [va-yomer]: "Let-us-surely-go-up [aloh na'aleh] and-possess [ve-yarashnu] it [otah], for [ki] we-are-surely-able [yakhol nukhal] to-[overcome]-it [lah]."

> **Key terms:** וַיַּהַס כָּלֵב אֶת־הָעָם אֶל־מֹשֶׁה (va-yahas Kalev et ha-am el Mosheh) = "and Caleb silenced the people toward Moses" — the verb הסה (hasah, "to hush/silence") in the hiphil: Caleb quiets the crowd that has begun to murmur. The phrase אֶל־מֹשֶׁה (el Mosheh) may mean "before Moses" (i.e., in Moses' presence) or "on behalf of Moses" (i.e., in support of Moses' mission). עָלֹה נַעֲלֶה (aloh na'aleh) = "we shall surely go up" — the infinitive absolute עָלֹה (aloh) intensifies the finite verb נַעֲלֶה (na'aleh): "going up, we shall go up!" — an emphatic declaration of confidence. וְיָרַשְׁנוּ אֹתָהּ (ve-yarashnu otah) = "and we shall possess/inherit it" — from ירשׁ (yarash, "to take possession of, inherit, dispossess"). The verb carries both senses: Israel will inherit the land and dispossess its current inhabitants. כִּי־יָכוֹל נוּכַל לָהּ (ki yakhol nukhal lah) = "for we are surely able to [overcome] it" — another infinitive absolute construction: יָכוֹל נוּכַל (yakhol nukhal) = "being able, we are able / we can certainly [do it]." Caleb's response is pure faith: the land is good, YHWH has promised it, therefore we can take it. He does not deny the difficulties but refuses to let them override the divine promise. **LXX:** καὶ κατεσιώπησεν Χαλεβ τὸν λαὸν πρὸς Μωυσῆν καὶ εἶπεν αὐτῷ· οὐχί, ἀλλὰ ἀναβάντες ἀναβησόμεθα καὶ κατακληρονομήσομεν αὐτήν, ὅτι δυνάμει δυνησόμεθα πρὸς αὐτούς ("and Caleb silenced the people before Moses and said to him: no, but going up we shall go up and inherit it, for in strength we shall prevail against them").

**31** And-the-men [ve-ha-anashim] who [asher] went-up [alu] with-him [immo] said [ameru]: "We-are-not-able [lo nukhal] to-go-up [la'alot] unto [el] the-people [ha-am], for [ki] [it is] stronger [khazaq] than-us [hu mimmenu]."

> **Key terms:** לֹא נוּכַל לַעֲלוֹת אֶל־הָעָם (lo nukhal la'alot el ha-am) = "we are not able to go up against the people" — the direct contradiction of Caleb's declaration in v. 30. Where Caleb said יָכוֹל נוּכַל ("we can certainly"), the ten say לֹא נוּכַל ("we cannot"). כִּי חָזָק הוּא מִמֶּנּוּ (ki khazaq hu mimmenu) = "for it/he [is] stronger than us" — the comparative מִמֶּנּוּ (mimmenu) means "more than us / stronger than we are." The pronoun הוּא (hu) may refer to "the people" (collective singular) or, as some Midrashim daringly interpret, to God himself — "it is stronger than Him" — meaning the spies implied that even YHWH could not overcome the Canaanites. Whether intended by the original text or not, the theological implication is clear: the spies' statement is a denial of YHWH's power. **LXX:** καὶ οἱ ἄνδρες οἱ συναναβάντες μετ᾽ αὐτοῦ εἶπαν· οὐκ ἀναβαίνομεν, ὅτι οὐ μὴ δυνώμεθα ἀναβῆναι πρὸς τὸ ἔθνος, ὅτι ἰσχυρότερόν ἐστιν μᾶλλον ἡμῶν ("and the men who went up with him said: we do not go up, for we are not at all able to go up against the nation, for it is much stronger than us").

**32** And-they-brought-out [va-yotsi'u] an-evil-report [dibbah] of-the-land [ha-arets] which [asher] they-had-spied-out [taru] it [otah] unto [el] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el], saying [le'mor]: "The-land [ha-arets] which [asher] we-passed-through [avarnu] in-it [vah] to-spy-it-out [la-tur] it [otah] — a-land [erets] eating [okhelet] its-inhabitants [yoshveiha] it [hi]; and-all [ve-khol] the-people [ha-am] which [asher] we-saw [ra'inu] in-its-midst [be-tokhah] [were] men of [anshei] measures [middot].

> **Key terms:** דִּבָּה (dibbah) = "evil report/slander" — from דבב (davav, a root connected to whispering, murmuring, defaming). The word carries a strong negative connotation: this is not merely a pessimistic assessment but a slanderous misrepresentation. The same word appears in Gen 37:2 (Joseph's "evil report" about his brothers) and Prov 10:18. דִּבַּת הָאָרֶץ (dibbat ha-arets) = "the evil report of the land" — the genitive construction: a defamatory report concerning the land. אֶרֶץ אֹכֶלֶת יוֹשְׁבֶיהָ הִוא (erets okhelet yoshveiha hi) = "a land eating/consuming its inhabitants [is] it" — a terrifying image: the land itself devours those who live in it. The participle אֹכֶלֶת (okhelet, "eating/consuming") personifies the land as a predatory force. This may refer to constant warfare among the peoples, to disease, to infertile soil (contradicting their own report in v. 27), or to a land so contested that its occupants are perpetually consumed by conflict. The image reverses the positive assessment: instead of a land "flowing with milk and honey," it is now a land that swallows its people. אַנְשֵׁי מִדּוֹת (anshei middot) = "men of measures/dimensions" — i.e., men of [great] stature, people of extraordinary physical size. מִדָּה (middah) = "measure/dimension/size." **LXX:** καὶ ἐξήνεγκαν ἔκστασιν τῆς γῆς ἣν κατεσκέψαντο αὐτὴν πρὸς τοὺς υἱοὺς Ισραηλ λέγοντες· τὴν γῆν ἣν παρήλθομεν αὐτὴν κατασκέψασθαι, γῆ κατέσθουσα τοὺς κατοικοῦντας ἐπ᾽ αὐτῆς ἐστίν ("and they brought out an ecstasy/horror of the land which they had spied out to the sons of Israel, saying: the land through which we passed to spy it out — a land consuming those dwelling upon it, it is") — the LXX renders דִּבָּה as ἔκστασιν (ekstasin, "horror/alarm"), interpreting the term as the report's effect rather than its moral quality.

**33** And-there [ve-sham] we-saw [ra'inu] the-Nephilim [ha-Nefilim], the-sons of [benei] Anak [Anaq] from [min] the-Nephilim [ha-Nefilim]; and-we-were [va-nehi] in-our-[own]-eyes [be-eineinu] as-grasshoppers [ka-khagavim], and-so [ve-khen] we-were [hayinu] in-their-eyes [be-eineihem].

> **Key terms:** הַנְּפִילִים (ha-Nefilim) = "the Nephilim" — the same term used in Gen 6:4 for the mysterious pre-flood figures ("the fallen ones" or "the ones who fall [upon others]"), from נפל (nafal, "to fall"). The spies' equation of the Anakim with the Nephilim is either a genuine identification (claiming that the legendary Nephilim survived the flood through the Anakite lineage) or, more likely, a hyperbolic comparison designed to terrify: "they were like the Nephilim of old." בְּנֵי עֲנָק מִן־הַנְּפִלִים (benei Anaq min ha-Nefilim) = "the sons of Anak from the Nephilim" — the preposition מִן (min, "from/of") indicates origin or descent: the Anakim come from (are descended from, or are of the same class as) the Nephilim. וַנְּהִי בְעֵינֵינוּ כַּחֲגָבִים (va-nehi ve-eineinu ka-khagavim) = "and we were in our [own] eyes as grasshoppers" — חָגָב (khagav) = "grasshopper/locust," one of the smallest creatures in the Israelite world. The comparison expresses radical self-diminishment: next to the giants, the spies felt like insects. This is the theological core of the failure — they measured themselves against the Canaanites rather than measuring the Canaanites against YHWH. וְכֵן הָיִינוּ בְּעֵינֵיהֶם (ve-khen hayinu be-eineihem) = "and so we were in their eyes" — the spies project their own self-assessment onto the Canaanites: "they saw us as grasshoppers too." This projection is unprovable — how would the spies know what the Canaanites thought of them? — and reveals the psychology of fear: those who see themselves as insignificant assume others see them the same way. The chapter ends on this note of despair, setting the stage for the communal crisis of chapter 14. **LXX:** καὶ ἐκεῖ ἑωράκαμεν τοὺς γίγαντας, καὶ ἦμεν ἐνώπιον αὐτῶν ὡσεὶ ἀκρίδες, ἀλλὰ καὶ οὕτως ἦμεν ἐνώπιον αὐτῶν ("and there we saw the giants, and we were before them as grasshoppers, and so we were before them") — the LXX renders הַנְּפִילִים as τοὺς γίγαντας (tous gigantas, "the giants"), its standard translation, which links the Nephilim to the Greek mythological γίγαντες (Gigantes) and established the "giants" tradition that dominated reception history.

## EMET's Textual Notes

### Manuscript Observations from the Leningrad Codex (pp. 162–164)

1. **Parashah boundary and chapter opening:** Numbers 13 opens the parashah שְׁלַח לְךָ (Shelakh lekha, "Send for yourself"), one of the major Torah portions. On page 162 of the Leningrad Codex, the new parashah begins following the setumah (ס) that closes the itinerary notice of 12:16. The opening words וַיְדַבֵּר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה לֵּאמֹר (va-yedabber YHWH el Mosheh le'mor) are clearly legible in the left column of folio 84 recto. The text continues across the verso (page 163) and onto page 164, where the chapter concludes. The three-column format of the Leningrad Codex is consistent throughout, with Masoretic marginal notes visible in the inter-column spaces and outer margins.

2. **The singular verb וַיָּבֹא in v. 22:** The Leningrad Codex clearly reads וַיָּבֹא (va-yavo, "and he came"), third-person masculine singular, despite the preceding plural subject implied by "they went up by the Negev." This singular form stands without qere correction in the manuscript, confirming that the Masoretes accepted it as the authoritative reading. The traditional interpretation (Sotah 34b) that Caleb alone went to Hebron to pray at the patriarchal tombs is grounded in this singular verb. The alternative explanation — that the singular is a collective or impersonal usage — is grammatically possible but less satisfying given the narrative specificity of the Hebron reference.

3. **The antiquarian note on Hebron (v. 22b):** The parenthetical statement that Hebron was built seven years before Zoan of Egypt (וְחֶבְרוֹן שֶׁבַע שָׁנִים נִבְנְתָה לִפְנֵי צֹעַן מִצְרָיִם) appears in the Leningrad Codex as part of the continuous text, with no scribal indication that it is a gloss or interpolation. The note's presence in a narrative about spying suggests it is an ancient editorial comment establishing Hebron's prestige — it is older than Egypt's famous Delta capital. This kind of antiquarian aside is characteristic of the Pentateuchal narrator (cf. Gen 13:10; 14:2–3; Deut 3:11).

4. **The spy roster (vv. 4–16) and Masoretic formatting:** The list of twelve names in the Leningrad Codex is written in continuous prose, not as a formatted list. Each entry follows the pattern לְמַטֵּה [tribe], [name] בֶּן [father's name]. There is no special spacing or line-breaking to set the roster apart from the surrounding narrative — the Masoretes treated it as integral prose. The names are clearly vocalized with their standard pointing.

5. **The word אֶפֶס (v. 28):** This pivotal adversative particle is clearly legible in the Leningrad Codex, beginning the negative portion of the spy report. The word stands at the start of a new clause with no unusual Masoretic marking, yet it functions as the narrative turning point — everything before it is affirmation of YHWH's promise, and everything after it is the language of fear.

6. **The phrase אֶרֶץ אֹכֶלֶת יוֹשְׁבֶיהָ (v. 32):** The metaphor "a land eating its inhabitants" is clearly written in the Leningrad Codex with the feminine participle אֹכֶלֶת (okhelet, "eating/consuming"), fully vocalized. The strangeness of this phrase — which contradicts the spies' own earlier report of a land flowing with milk and honey — is preserved without scribal comment, suggesting that the Masoretes understood the contradiction as part of the narrative's theological design: the evil report distorts reality.

### Observations from Codex Vaticanus (pp. 121–122)

7. **LXX versification divergence:** The Vaticanus preserves a slightly different arrangement of the chapter's contents compared to the MT. The LXX version of Numbers 13 includes material that the MT places in different order, particularly in the transition from the spy list to the charge and from the charge to the journey. The overall content is substantially the same, but the sequence of certain verses differs, suggesting that the LXX Vorlage (the Hebrew text underlying the Greek translation) may have had a different textual arrangement than what is preserved in the Masoretic tradition. The translation above follows the MT versification of the Leningrad Codex.

8. **LXX rendering of דִּבָּה as ἔκστασιν (v. 32):** The Vaticanus reads ἔκστασιν (ekstasin, "horror/consternation/ecstasy") where the MT has דִּבָּה (dibbah, "evil report/slander"). This is a significant interpretive divergence. The MT's דִּבָּה focuses on the moral quality of the report — it is slander, a defamatory distortion of reality. The LXX's ἔκστασις focuses on the effect of the report — it produces horror and alarm. The two readings are not contradictory (a slanderous report produces horror), but they emphasize different dimensions of the same event.

9. **LXX rendering of הַנְּפִילִים as γίγαντες (v. 33):** The Vaticanus renders the Hebrew הַנְּפִילִים (ha-Nefilim, "the Nephilim / the fallen ones") with τοὺς γίγαντας (tous gigantas, "the giants"), following the standard LXX translation of this term (cf. Gen 6:4 LXX). This Greek rendering had enormous influence on reception history, establishing the "giants" interpretation that dominates Christian tradition. The Hebrew term is more ambiguous — נפילים could mean "fallen ones" (from נפל, "to fall") or "those who cause [others] to fall" — but the LXX translators resolved the ambiguity in favor of physical gigantism.

10. **LXX addition of the Hivite (v. 29):** The Vaticanus includes ὁ Ευαῖος (ho Euaios, "the Hivite") in the list of Canaanite peoples occupying the hill country, where the MT has the Jebusite. This is a common variant within the traditional "nations of Canaan" lists, which fluctuate between five, six, and seven peoples across different biblical texts (cf. Exod 3:8, 17; 23:23; 33:2; 34:11; Deut 7:1; Josh 3:10; 9:1; 11:3). The variant does not affect the theological point — the land is occupied by multiple powerful peoples — but it illustrates the fluidity of these ethnic-list traditions.

### Theological and Structural Notes

11. **The initiative question — YHWH's command vs. the people's request:** The most significant structural tension in Numbers 13 is the relationship between vv. 1–2 and the parallel account in Deuteronomy 1:22–23. In the MT of Numbers, YHWH initiates the mission: "Send for yourself men" (v. 2). In Deuteronomy, Moses recalls that the people approached him with the request. The traditional reconciliation (following Rashi and the Midrash) reads שְׁלַח לְךָ (shelakh lekha) as permissive rather than imperative: "Send for yourself" — meaning "if you wish, send; I am not commanding you." On this reading, the people proposed the mission (Deut 1:22), Moses approved (Deut 1:23), and YHWH authorized it (Num 13:1–2) without strictly commanding it. The ethical dative לְךָ (lekha) is the grammatical key: it signals discretion, not obligation.

12. **The function of the spy roster (vv. 4–16):** The list of twelve names, one per tribe, establishes several narrative purposes. First, it demonstrates that the mission was representative — all Israel participated through its leaders. Second, the names themselves carry theological significance: several contain divine elements (Ammi'el = "God is my kinsman"; Gaddi'el = "God is my fortune"; Ge'u'el = "majesty of God"; Mikha'el = "who is like God?"), reminding the reader that the spies went out bearing names that confessed faith in God. Third, the list makes clear that only two of twelve — Caleb (v. 6) and Hoshea/Joshua (v. 8) — will remain faithful. The ten-to-two ratio is itself significant: the minority of faith against the majority of fear.

13. **The renaming of Joshua (v. 16) — theological arming:** Moses' renaming of Hoshe'a ("he saves") to Yehoshu'a ("YHWH saves") prefixes the divine name to the root of salvation. This is not merely an honorific but a theological confession embedded in a personal name. By giving his apprentice a name that affirms YHWH as the agent of deliverance, Moses arms Joshua with the very truth that the ten spies will deny: it is not Israel's military capacity that determines the outcome, but YHWH's saving action. The name change occurs just before the mission, suggesting prophetic foresight — Moses knows that what the spies will encounter will test whether they believe that YHWH saves.

14. **The adversative אֶפֶס (v. 28) — the anatomy of unbelief:** The word אֶפֶס ("however/but") in v. 28 is arguably the most consequential single word in the chapter. Everything preceding it is true and faith-affirming: the land is good, it flows with milk and honey, here is the fruit. Everything following it is also observationally true: the people are strong, the cities are fortified, the Anakim are there. The sin of the ten spies is not in what they observed but in the word that connects their observations: "however." The adversative particle subordinates YHWH's promise to human assessment. It says, in effect: "God promised — but the obstacles are too great." Faith would have said: "The obstacles are great — but God promised." The spies' failure is a failure of conjunction: they used the wrong connective word.

15. **"A land eating its inhabitants" (v. 32) — the escalation of slander:** The phrase אֶרֶץ אֹכֶלֶת יוֹשְׁבֶיהָ ("a land eating its inhabitants") directly contradicts the spies' own earlier testimony in v. 27 ("flowing with milk and honey"). Within five verses, the same men describe the same land as both overflowing with abundance and devouring its people. This internal contradiction reveals the nature of דִּבָּה (dibbah, "evil report/slander"): it is not a mistaken assessment but a motivated distortion. The slander intensifies as the report progresses: the people are strong (v. 28), the land devours its inhabitants (v. 32), and we were like grasshoppers (v. 33). Each stage moves further from observation and deeper into fear-driven fabrication.

16. **"We were in our own eyes as grasshoppers" (v. 33) — the psychology of faithlessness:** The final verse contains two confessions that reveal the inner logic of the spies' failure. First, "we were in our own eyes as grasshoppers" (va-nehi ve-eineinu ka-khagavim) — this is a self-assessment, not an observation about the enemy. The spies' problem is not the size of the Canaanites but the smallness of their own self-image. They forgot that they were the people of YHWH, redeemed from Egypt, recipients of covenant and promise. Second, "and so we were in their eyes" (ve-khen hayinu be-eineihem) — this is a projection: the spies assume that others see them as they see themselves. The sentence is epistemically impossible — the spies cannot know what the Canaanites thought — and reveals that fear creates its own evidence. Those who see themselves as insignificant construct a world in which everyone else agrees. The chapter thus ends not with a military assessment but with a psychological and theological diagnosis: Israel's failure to enter the land begins with a failure to see themselves as God sees them.
